perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to take all necessary precautions to maintain the confidentiality of the Non -Public Information and to prevent unauthorized access to it.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to take reasonable precautions to maintain the confidentiality of the Non -Public Information and to discourage unauthorized access to it.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'all necessary precautions' with 'reasonable precautions' and 'prevent' with 'discourage' weakens the obligation, introducing ambiguity about the required level of security. This introduces ambiguity because 'reasonable' is subjective and 'discourage' does not guarantee prevention.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.81.5 mandates specific, reasonable security procedures and practices to protect personal information. The modified text's use of 'reasonable precautions' and 'discourage' is vague and doesn't guarantee the implementation of robust measures required by the law, such as data encryption or access controls, potentially leading to non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798.81.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect personal information, potentially contradicting California Civil Code § 1798.81.5. The original text requires 'all necessary precautions' to 'prevent unauthorized access,' implying a higher standard of security. The changed text uses 'reasonable precautions' and 'discourage,' which are less stringent. Snippet 1 states the legislature's intent is 'to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected' and encourages businesses 'to provide reasonable security for that information.' Replacing 'all necessary' with 'reasonable' introduces ambiguity and may not meet the standard of reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information as specified by law, especially since 'discourage' does not guarantee prevention. The change creates ambiguity in the level of security required, and reasonable precautions could be interpreted as less secure than all necessary precautions.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Data Recipient shares Non -Public Information WECC Confidentiality Agreement 2 with a contractor pursuant to this provision, Data Recipient agrees to be responsible and jointly and severally liable for any breach of confidentiality by that contractor
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Data Recipient shares Non -Public Information WECC Confidentiality Agreement 2 with a contractor pursuant to this provision, Data Recipient agrees to consider being responsible for any breach of confidentiality by that contractor
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to be responsible and jointly and severally liable' to 'agrees to consider being responsible' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the legal obligation. It's no longer a definite commitment to liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714 establishes the principle of responsibility for injury occasioned to another by want of ordinary care or skill in the management of property or person. By only agreeing to 'consider being responsible,' the modified text creates uncertainty and contradicts the general principle of liability for negligence and breach of contract, undermining the protections for confidential information under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the legal obligation by changing 'agrees to be responsible and jointly and severally liable' to 'agrees to consider being responsible.' This introduces ambiguity about the Data Recipient's liability for a contractor's breach of confidentiality. California Civil Code § 1714(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that everyone is responsible for injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill. The changed text contradicts this principle by creating uncertainty about whether the Data Recipient will actually be responsible, undermining the protection for confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient may disclose Non -Public Information to a governmental authority as required by law, provided that to the extent permitted by law: i. Data Recipient notifies WECC as soon as reasonably possible of the required disclosure; ii. Data Recipient does not disclose the Non -Public Information until WECC has had a reasonable opportunity to respon d to the required disclosure; and iii. Data Recipient cooperates with WECC as reasonably requested by WECC to protect WECC’s interests in the Non -Public Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient may disclose Non -Public Information to a governmental authority as required by law, provided that: i. Data Recipient attempts to notify WECC of the required disclosure; ii. Data Recipient considers WECC's opportunity to respond to the required disclosure; and iii. Data Recipient reflects on cooperating with WECC to protect WECC’s interests in the Non -Public Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces mandatory actions with vague intentions. 'Notifies as soon as reasonably possible' becomes 'attempts to notify', 'does not disclose until WECC has had a reasonable opportunity' becomes 'considers WECC's opportunity', and 'cooperates' becomes 'reflects on cooperating'. This creates ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1905 prohibits the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information by federal employees or those acting on behalf of the government. While this agreement isn't directly about federal employees, the principle of protecting confidential information disclosed to government entities is relevant. By weakening the notification and cooperation requirements, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, potentially conflicting with the spirit and intent of laws like 18 U.S.C. § 1905, which aims to prevent the misuse of sensitive data.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 93 / § 1905 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1905 : Disclosure of confidential information generally Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 93-PUBLIC OFFICERS AND EMPLOYEES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §1905. Disclosure of confidential information generally Whoever, being an officer or employee of the United States or of any department or agency thereof, any person acting on behalf of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, or agent of the Department of Justice as defined in the Antitrust Civil Process Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1311–1314 ), or being an employee of a private sector org
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligations of the Data Recipient, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates specific actions ('notifies as soon as reasonably possible,' 'does not disclose until WECC has had a reasonable opportunity,' 'cooperates'), while the changed text replaces these with vague intentions ('attempts to notify,' 'considers WECC's opportunity,' 'reflects on cooperating'). This shift from mandatory actions to mere considerations undermines the protection of Non-Public Information, especially in the context of potential governmental disclosure as alluded to in the Law Explanation concerning 18 U.S.C. § 1905, even though that law does not directly apply. The essence of protecting confidential information is compromised when obligations become discretionary. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to 18 USC 1905, focuses on the 'Disclosure of confidential information generally'. While the agreement isn't about federal employees, it demonstrates the legal concern with the disclosure of such information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be fully enforceable by WECC and any entity whose Non -Public Information is not treated in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be enforceable by WECC and entities whose Non -Public Information is not ideally treated according to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be fully enforceable' to 'may be enforceable' makes the enforceability discretionary. Replacing 'in accordance with' with 'ideally treated' introduces a subjective standard, further weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1549 defines a contract as an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing, implying a clear and enforceable obligation. By changing 'shall be fully enforceable' to 'may be enforceable', the agreement's enforceability becomes uncertain, undermining the contractual obligation and potentially rendering the contract unenforceable under California law. The term 'ideally treated' is ambiguous and does not provide a clear standard for compliance, further conflicting with contract law principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1549 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Definition [1549 - 1550.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 1549. A contract is an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1549. The original contract states the agreement 'shall be fully enforceable,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text alters this to 'may be enforceable,' making enforceability discretionary, and replaces 'in accordance with' with the subjective term 'ideally treated'. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1549 states that 'A contract is an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing.' By making the enforceability uncertain and introducing subjective standards, the changed text undermines the 'certain thing' aspect of the agreement, creating ambiguity where the law requires clarity and enforceability. The change weakens the legal obligation, making it more difficult to enforce, and thus contradicts the law.
